rows.desc: Sets the name that will be attached to all commits globally on your machine
Sets the email address that will be attached to all commits globally
Sets the default text editor used by Git for commit messages and interactive commands
Enables colorized output in the terminal for easier reading of Git responses
Displays all current Git configuration settings from system, global, and local levels
Creates a shortcut alias for a longer Git command to speed up workflow
Configures how Git handles line endings between different operating systems
Sets the default behavior when pulling; true makes pull use rebase instead of merge
Sets the default branch name (e.g., main) when initializing a new repository
Applies configuration at the system level affecting all users on the machine
Applies configuration only to the current repository, overriding global settings
User-level configuration file stored in the home directory containing global Git settings
Initializes a new empty Git repository in the current directory, creating a .git folder
Creates a new Git repository inside a specified directory
Creates a bare repository with no working directory; used as a central remote repository
Downloads a remote repository to your local machine including all branches and history
Clones a remote repository into a specifically named local directory
Creates a shallow clone with only the last n commits, reducing download size
Clones the repository and checks out a specific branch instead of the default
Clones only the specified branch without fetching other branches
Clones the repository along with all nested submodules recursively
Shows the current state of the working directory and staging area with tracked/untracked files
Stages a specific file's changes to be included in the next commit
Stages all modified and new files in the current directory and subdirectories
Stages all changes including modifications, additions, and deletions across entire repo
Interactively stages portions (hunks) of files, allowing partial staging of changes
Stages modifications and deletions of tracked files only; does not add new untracked files
Removes a file from the working directory and stages its deletion for the next commit
Removes a file from the staging area only, keeping it in the working directory (untrack it)
Renames or moves a file and stages the change in one command
Discards unstaged changes in a file, restoring it to the last committed state
Unstages a file from the staging area without discarding its working directory changes
Removes untracked files and directories from the working directory forcefully
File listing patterns of files and directories Git should ignore and not track
Opens editor to write a commit message and creates a snapshot of staged changes
Creates a commit with the given message inline without opening an editor
Stages all tracked modified files and commits them in a single command
Modifies the most recent commit by changing its message or adding new staged changes
Adds staged changes to the last commit without changing the commit message
Creates a commit even when there are no staged changes; useful for triggering CI/CD
Creates a GPG-signed commit to verify the identity of the author
Standard commit message format: type(scope): description — e.g., feat, fix, docs, chore
Creates a fixup commit to be automatically squashed into a previous commit during rebase
Lists all local branches; the current branch is highlighted with an asterisk
Creates a new branch at the current commit without switching to it
Deletes a branch that has already been merged; prevents accidental data loss
Force deletes a branch regardless of its merge status
Renames a branch from old name to new name
Lists all local and remote tracking branches
Lists all remote tracking branches fetched from the remote
Lists branches that have been merged into the current branch
Lists branches that have NOT been merged into the current branch
Sets the upstream (tracking) remote branch for the current local branch
Switches to an existing branch; modern replacement for checkout for branch switching
Creates a new branch and immediately switches to it
Switches to an existing branch and updates the working directory
Creates a new branch and switches to it in one command
Moves HEAD to a specific commit, entering detached HEAD state
Checks out the commit a tag points to, entering detached HEAD state
Discards uncommitted changes in a specific file, restoring it from last commit
Checks out a specific file from another branch into the current working directory
Creates a new branch with no commit history, starting fresh with a clean slate
State where HEAD points directly to a commit instead of a branch; commits may be lost
Integrates changes from the specified branch into the current branch
Moves the branch pointer forward when no diverging commits exist; no merge commit created
Creates a merge commit when branches have diverged, combining two histories
Forces creation of a merge commit even when a fast-forward is possible
Combines all commits from a branch into one staged change without creating a merge commit
Cancels an in-progress merge and restores the pre-merge state
Occurs when the same lines are changed differently in two branches being merged
Opens a visual merge tool to resolve conflicts interactively
Specifies a merge strategy such as ours, theirs, or recursive
Merging more than two branches simultaneously in a single merge commit
Re-applies commits from the current branch on top of another branch for a linear history
Interactive rebase to edit, reorder, squash, or drop the last n commits
Continues the rebase process after resolving conflicts in the current commit
Cancels an in-progress rebase and returns to the original branch state
Skips the current conflicting commit and continues with the next one
Rebases a range of commits onto a different base commit
Combining multiple commits into one during interactive rebase using the squash option
Fetches remote changes and replays local commits on top instead of creating a merge commit
Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared public branch
Lists all remote connections configured for the current repository
Lists all remotes with their fetch and push URLs
Adds a new remote connection with a given name and URL
Removes a remote connection from the repository
Renames an existing remote connection
Changes the URL of an existing remote connection
Displays detailed information about a remote including tracked branches
Removes stale remote-tracking branches that no longer exist on the remote
Default name Git gives to the remote a repository was cloned from
Conventional name for the original repository when working with forks
Downloads new commits and branches from the remote without merging into local branches
Fetches all updates from a specific remote repository
Fetches updates from all configured remote repositories
Fetches and removes stale remote-tracking branches that no longer exist on the remote
Fetches from the tracked remote branch and merges into the current local branch
Fetches and merges a specific remote branch into the current branch
Uploads local commits from the current branch to its tracked remote branch
Pushes a local branch to a specific remote repository
Pushes branch and sets it as the tracking upstream for future push/pull
Overwrites remote branch history with local history; use with caution on shared branches
Safer force push that fails if someone else has pushed since your last fetch
Pushes all local tags to the remote repository
Deletes a branch on the remote repository
Temporarily saves uncommitted changes and reverts the working directory to a clean state
Creates a stash with a descriptive message for easier identification later
Shows all stashed entries with their index and description
Applies the most recent stash and removes it from the stash list
Applies the most recent stash without removing it from the stash list
Applies a specific stash by its index without removing it
Deletes a specific stash entry from the stash list
Removes all stash entries permanently
Creates a new branch from the stash and applies the stash to it
Stashes untracked files along with modified tracked files
Stashes all files including untracked and ignored files
Shows the full commit history with hash, author, date, and message
Displays a compact log with one line per commit showing short hash and message
Draws a text-based graph of branch and merge history alongside the log
Shows commits from all branches not just the current one
Limits the log output to the specified number of most recent commits
Filters commits to show only those made by a specific author
Filters commits within a specific date range
Shows commits whose messages match a given search pattern
Shows commits that added or removed a specific string (pickaxe search)
Shows only commits that modified a specific file
Shows unstaged differences between the working directory and the staging area
Shows differences between the staging area and the last commit
Compares the tips of two branches showing what is different
Displays the changes introduced by a specific commit along with its metadata
Shows who last modified each line of a file and which commit made the change
Summarizes git log output grouped by author with commit counts
Creates a new commit that undoes changes from a specific commit; safe for public branches
Applies the revert changes to working directory without immediately committing
Moves HEAD back n commits but keeps all changes staged in the index
Moves HEAD back n commits and unstages changes but keeps them in working directory (default)
Moves HEAD back n commits and discards all changes permanently in working directory
Removes a file from the staging area without discarding its working directory changes
Discards unstaged changes in a file restoring it from the index or last commit
Records all movements of HEAD allowing recovery of lost commits after reset or rebase
Applies a specific commit from another branch onto the current branch
Applies a range of commits from one branch onto the current branch
Cancels an in-progress cherry-pick and restores the previous state
Lists all existing tags in the repository
Creates a lightweight tag pointing to the current commit
Creates an annotated tag with a message, tagger name, email, and date
Creates a tag pointing to a specific past commit
Deletes a local tag by name
Pushes a specific tag to the remote repository
Pushes all local tags to the remote at once
Removes a tag from the remote repository
A simple pointer to a commit with no extra metadata; just a name and a commit hash
A full Git object with message, tagger info, and date; recommended for releases
Tag naming convention: v<major>.<minor>.<patch> e.g., v1.4.2 used in releases
Begins a binary search through commit history to find the commit that introduced a bug
Marks a commit as good (bug not present) to narrow the bisect range
Marks a commit as bad (bug present) to narrow the bisect range
Ends the bisect session and returns to the original branch
Automates bisecting by running a script that returns 0 for good and non-zero for bad
Searches for a pattern in tracked files across the repository
Searches for a pattern and shows matching line numbers
Finds commits that introduced or removed a specific string in the codebase
Shows blame for a specific range of lines in a file
Adds an external repository as a submodule inside the current repository
Initializes submodule configuration from .gitmodules into .git/config
Clones and checks out submodules to the commit recorded in the parent repository
Updates submodules to the latest commit on their tracking branches
Runs a shell command in each submodule directory
Clones a repo and automatically initializes and updates all its submodules
Unregisters a submodule, removing its entry from .git/config
Configuration file that lists submodule paths and their remote URLs
Creates a new working directory linked to the same repository for a different branch
Lists all working trees associated with the current repository
Removes a worktree directory and its reference from the repository
Cleans up stale worktree references that no longer exist on disk
Prevents a worktree from being pruned if it is on a removable device
Hook that runs before a commit is created; used for linting, formatting, or tests
Hook that validates the commit message format before the commit is finalized
Hook that modifies the default commit message before the editor opens
Hook that runs after a commit is created; used for notifications or logging
Hook that runs before git push; used to run tests or block pushes of certain branches
Server-side hook that runs before refs are updated; used to enforce policies
Server-side hook that runs after a push is accepted; used to trigger deployments
Hook that runs after a successful merge; useful for installing dependencies automatically
Hook that runs before a rebase begins; can be used to prevent rebasing certain branches
Popular npm tool to manage Git hooks in JavaScript projects via package.json
All developers commit directly to a single main branch similar to SVN workflow
Each feature is developed in a dedicated branch and merged to main when complete
Uses main, develop, feature, release, and hotfix branches for structured release management
Each developer forks the official repo and submits pull requests to contribute
All developers integrate small changes directly to the main branch multiple times per day
Request to merge a branch into another; enables code review before integration
Process of examining pull request changes by peers before merging to ensure quality
Repository settings requiring PR approvals and status checks before merging to protected branches
File specifying which users or teams own specific files and must review PRs affecting them
Git object that stores the raw contents of a file without the filename or metadata
Git object that represents a directory, mapping filenames to blob or other tree objects
Git object pointing to a tree with metadata including author, date, message, and parent commits
Git object for annotated tags, storing the tag name, message, and reference to a commit
40-character hash uniquely identifying every Git object; Git 2.29+ supports SHA-256
A pointer to the currently checked-out branch or commit in the repository
Intermediate area where changes are prepared before being committed to the repository
Hidden directory containing all repository data including objects, refs, config, and hooks
Compressed collection of Git objects that reduces storage size and speeds up transfers
Runs garbage collection to clean up unnecessary files and optimize the local repository
Inspects the type and content of any Git object by its hash
Lists the contents of a tree object showing blobs and subtrees with their hashes
Computes the SHA-1 hash of a file and optionally writes it as a Git object
